coredata.dc:title: Role of carbon quantum dots in a strategic approach to prepare pristine Zn2SnO4 and enhance photocatalytic activity under direct sunlight
coredata.dc:creator: G, Preethi
Pillai, R.
Ramdas, Balan
Ramamoorthy, S.
Patil, Balu
Lekshmi, I.C.
Mohan Kumar, P.
Rangaraj, Lingappa
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are used in a strategic approach to obtain pristine mesoporous Zn2SnO4 (ZTO) and enhanced photodegradation of methylene blue (MB) dye. The Zn2SnO4/carbon quantum dots (ZTO/C) was hydrothermally prepared using mineralizer, Na2CO3 and quantified amount of sugarcane juice. The mesoporous nature and high surface area of ZTO and ZTO/C was revealed from Brunauer, Emmett and Teller (BET). The close overlapping of CQD with ZTO was evident from the HRTEM and Raman spectra. The MB dye degradation study employing ZTO and ZTO/C catalyst under direct sunlight irradiation showed 84 % and 95 % degradation in 150 min respectively. The presence of CQDs in ZTO/C nanocomposites enhanced the transportation of photoinduced electrons at the interface between ZTO and CQDs, whereas holes stayed in the valence band of ZTO, suppressing the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers during photocatalysis. This study reveals that ZTO/C as a possible candidate for sunlight-driven photocatalysis and environmental remediation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zn2SnO4
Carbon quantum dots
Nanocomposite
Photocatalyst
Dye degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of ZTO/C and pristine ZTO
Photodegradation of methylene blue under sunlight irradiation
Raman spectroscopy and diffuse reflectance studies
BET surface area and pore distribution
Reusability and Scavengers studies of the photocatalyst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Powder XRD patterns of hydrothermal prepared compounds at 180 °C for 16 h using (a) 2Zn:1Sn (b) 2Zn:1Sn: 4Na2CO3 (c) 2 Zn:1Sn: 4Na2CO3 and sugar cane juice, (d) calcined product at 600 °C of the nanocomposite prepared using 2 Zn:1Sn: 4Na2CO3 and sugar cane juice.
Hydrothermal prepared ZTO/C, ZTO/ZnO and ZTO compounds (a) Raman Spectrum and (b) Kubleka-Munk plot.
Electron microscopy a) & b) HRTEM of ZTO, c) SAED pattern of ZTO d) & e) HRTEM of ZTO/C and f) EDS pattern.
ZTO/C and ZTO of (a) Nitrogen adsorption−desorption isotherm (b) pore size distribution curve.
(a) Ct/C0 versus time for ZTO and ZTO/C nanocomposite under sunlight. (b) lnC0/Ct versus time plot for ZTO and ZTO/C nanocomposite, (c) reusability studies and (d) Scavanger studies.
Schematic diagram of plausible mechanism of MB dye degradation by ZTO/C photocatalyst.
Surface area, pore volume and pore diameter of the prepared photocatalysts.
Comparison of MB dye degradation by carbon supported catalyst in the visible light irradation.

head.ce:title: Role of carbon quantum dots in a strategic approach to prepare pristine Zn2SnO4 and enhance photocatalytic activity under direct sunlight

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CMR Institute of Technology (NAAC A++ Accredited)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present work, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are used in a strategic approach to obtain pristine mesoporous Zn2SnO4 (ZTO) and enhanced photodegradation of methylene blue (MB) dye. The Zn2SnO4/carbon quantum dots (ZTO/C) was hydrothermally prepared using mineralizer, Na2CO3 and quantified amount of sugarcane juice. The mesoporous nature and high surface area of ZTO and ZTO/C was revealed from Brunauer, Emmett and Teller (BET). The close overlapping of CQD with ZTO was evident from the HRTEM and Raman spectra. The MB dye degradation study employing ZTO and ZTO/C catalyst under direct sunlight irradiation showed 84 % and 95 % degradation in 150 min respectively. The presence of CQDs in ZTO/C nanocomposites enhanced the transportation of photoinduced electrons at the interface between ZTO and CQDs, whereas holes stayed in the valence band of ZTO, suppressing the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers during photocatalysis. This study reveals that ZTO/C as a possible candidate for sunlight-driven photocatalysis and environmental remediation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapid urbanisation and growth of modern technologies have resulted in emerge of huge number of industries, which has staggeringly improved the quality of life. On the contrary, harmful effluents from the industries have become a major concern to the environment protection [1]. Especially, dyes and pigment effluents from leather, textile, paper and paint industries invariably causing serious imbalance in the aquatic environment [2]. The conventional methods practised for decontamination of these effluents before letting out to the environment are adsorption, reduction, reverse osmosis and related waste water treatment plants. In the recent, Advanced oxidation process (AOP) based photocatalysis has been widely studied due to its ability to degrade dyes under the natural sunlight or ultraviolet -visible light source without any form of carbon footprint [3]. There are various kinds of photocatalytic materials have been studied which includes metal oxides, nanocomposites, carbon-based nanostructures, metal organic frameworks in order to enhance the degradation efficiency with enhanced catalytic performance [4] [5,6].

Among various complex metal oxides, Zn2SnO4 (ZTO) is an important ternary n-type semiconductor oxides possessing high optoelectronic properties, chemical sensitivity, good electron conductivity and mobility. These properties make them an ideal candidate for versatile applications like photocatalyst, gas sensing, electrode material in rechargeable batteries, solar cells and supercapacitors [7–11]. There are several conventional techniques for synthesis of ZTO such as high-temperature solid state reactions, co-precipitation, thermal evaporation, chemical vapour deposition methods, ball milling etc. [12–15] [16]. However, these methods involve high temperature or complex process to obtain pure and crystalline form of ZTO. Hydrothermal method in comparison with the conventional methods of preparation of ZTO is advantages due to facile, low temperature and environmental benign approach [17,18]. Hydrothermal approach is commonly reported method to prepare ZTO in various morphological forms such as nanowires [19], nanorods [20], irregular particles [21], well-defined polyhedra [10], and hierarchical cube-like microstructures constructed with nanoplates. These method mandates the use of mineralizer such as NaOH [11], hydrazine hydrate [20], Na2CO3 [22] to obtain the pure form of ZTO. Nevertheless, post using mineraliser, obtaining the pure phase is still a challenge and would result in biphase [11,23]. Carbon compounds are well-known reducing agents which helps in surface modification of materials [24] [25] and possibly can suppress the formation of other phase [26].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are gaining significance owing to its larger surface area, high porosity, easy availability and low cost, excellent biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and good water dispersibility, which enables it to find suitable slot in wide range of applications [27–29]. Recently carbon quantum dots modified semiconducting materials have emerged as efficient heterogeneous catalysts owing to the amplified physiochemical properties compared to the parent material [30]. Another crucial parameter is selection of suitable carbon source which could yield better surface property for CQDs for the development of cost-effective catalyst for industrial effluents treatment. Taking into consideration of all the challenges. Herein, we report the synthesis of carbon quantum dots modified Zn2SnO4 at temperature of 180 °C for soaking period of 16 h using facile hydrothermal synthesis method. The study further reports a strategic approach to derive pristine Zn2SnO4 from the Zn2SnO4/C composite. The prepared compounds were characterized using several techniques for structural, morphological and band gap engineering. Further these prepared materials were subjected to study photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue dye which is one of the common water pollutants.

The Zinc acetate (ZnC4H6O4), sodium stannate hydrate (Na2SnO3.3H2O) and Na2CO3 procured from SD fine chemicals, India was used without further purification. Sugar cane juice freshly extracted from sugarcane and filtered using Whatmann filter (No 1) paper was used for the experiments. The as-prepared and calcined powders were characterized to study the phase purity using Powder XRD -Bruker D2 Phaser instrument with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) in the 2θ range of 10–60°. Scanning electron microscopic (SEM) images were recorded using JEOL JCM 6000Plus and HRTEM images using TALOS F200S G2 instrument to study the morphology and particle size. Reflectance spectrum was recorded to study the optical band structure employing LAMBDA 365 UV–Visible spectrophotometer. Raman spectra was recorded using PeakSeeker ProTM Raman spectrometer fitted with 785 nm wavelength laser. BET surface area study using Nova station B (Quantachrome instrument), prior to the measurements all the samples were degassed at a temperature of 200 °C for 6 h. Throughout the experiment double distilled water was used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ZTO/C and pristine ZTO
ce:section.ce:para: The ZTO/C was prepared by hydrothermal method. The typical synthesis procedure involves dropwise addition of 20 ml each of Zinc acetate (0.5 M), sodium stannate (0.25 M) and Na2CO3 (1 M) into a 250 ml beaker with constant stirring to form a white slurry. To this solution, a preoptimized 3 ml of sugarcane juice was added and further stirred for 30 min. The resulting slurry was transferred to a teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave with a capacity of 80 ml, sealed & heated to 180 °C in a hot air oven for 16 h and allowed to cool room temperature. The formed product was carefully cleaned with copious amount of distilled water before being dried overnight at 80 °C. The powder obtained was ZTO/C with a dirty white in colour. This powder was further heat treated to 600 °C for 3 h in a muffle furnace to obtain pure white colour powder, which was pristine ZTO confirmed by XRD and Raman spectra. To further elucidate the role of CQDS and mineraliser, the synthesis was carried out under same conditions without the mineraliser and carbon source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodegradation of methylene blue under sunlight irradiation
ce:section.ce:para:
Photocatalytic activity of the prepared photocatalysts, ZTO/C and pristine ZTO were studied for degradation of methylene blue (MB) as a model dye under direct sunlight irradiation. In the present experiment, an optimized amount of 200 mg of the prepared photocatalyst was suspended in 200 ml of 5 ppm MB dye solution. The suspension was maintained in the dark condition on constant stirring for 30 min to attain adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Subsequently, the suspension was kept under direct sunlight between 11 am–1 pm in the open play ground, CMRIT, India for photocatalytic degradation. Approximately 3 ml of dye solution was removed from the suspension at regular intervals during this procedure to monitor the dye content by recording the absorption spectra with a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Lambda 365, Perkin Elmer spectrophotometer). The dye's characteristic absorption band maximum was at λmax = 664 nm, which was related to the auxochrome group of methylene blue dye. The following equation was used to calculate dye degradation:
(1)
% Degradation =
C 0
−
C t
C 0
× 100
where C0 and Ct were the absorbance values of the solution at 0 and t minutes respectively.

The recovered photocatalysts were studied for recyclability for three cycles under same conditions. The scavenger test was carried out for photocatalysts to identify the reactive species involved in this photocatalysis mechanism by the addition of scavengers such as BQ, KI, KBrO3, and IPA for.O2, h+, e− and. OH respectively.

PXRD pattern of the as-prepared and calcined powders are shown in Fig. 1 . XRD pattern (Fig. 1a) of the compound prepared using only the precursors i.e. without mineralizer depicts major ZnSn(OH)6 phase with small trace of ZTO. The compound prepared with the Na2CO3 mineralizer (shown in Fig. 1b) showed major ZTO with a trace of ZnO phase [23]. Similar, ZnO biphase is reported by Najam M et.al [31], and Miyauchi et.al [32] during the preparation of ZTO using NH4OH and NaOH mineralizers respectively. Whereas, Annamalai et.al reported mono-phasic ZTO prepared using SnCl4 and Zn(ac)2 starting materials with the Na2CO3 mineralizer in hydrothermal condition [22]. Hence, this elicit the role of hydroxide formed in the formation of ZnO, Herein, NaOH formed from the Na2SnO3.3H2O could be a potential reason for the formation of ZnO bi-phase.

Fig. 1c, XRD pattern of the compound prepared with the aid of Na2CO3 mineralizer and sugar cane juice as a source of carbon shown good agreement with the standard data of ZTO (JCPDS card No. 24-1470) whereas no carbon diffraction peak was observed. But, greyish colour of the composite (shown in the inset image) confirms the presence of amorphous carbon which upon further characterisation revealed to be quantum dots. CQDs are also well-known reducing agent [33] which attributes that the CQDs formed from the sugarcane juice suppressed the formation of ZnO yielding to obtain pure form of ZTO. XRD pattern of the calcined product of as-prepared ZTO/C at 600 °C is shown in the Fig. 1d. The calcined compound was white in colour shown in the insert image. The entire diffraction peaks were well matches with the ZTO standard JCPDS data, file no 24-1470 to reveal the single-phase cubic spinel oxides with space group Fd-3 m (227). The diffraction peaks obtained at the peak position 17.59°, 28.97°, 34.09°, 35.62°, 41.48°, 45.44°, 51.47°, 54.67° were assigned to (111), (220), (311), (222), (400), (331), (422), and (511) planes. The average crystallite size was calculated employing Debye-Scherrer formula (Eq. (2)) to be 25 nm.
(2)
D = Kλ / β
cosθ
where, constant K = 0.94, λ is the wavelength of the x-ray used i,e Cu Kα = 0.15406 nm, θ is the Bragg angle of diffraction and β the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of diffracted peak.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Raman spectroscopy and diffuse reflectance studies
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2a shows the Raman spectra of pristine ZTO and ZTO/C photocatalysts. The spectra depicts strong Raman peaks at 527 cm−1 and 667 cm−1 corresponding to ZTO [34] which could be assigned to F2g symmetric bending of oxygen atoms in the M-O bonds of the MO6 octahedra (M = Zn or Sn) and A1g symmetric vibration of the ZnO bonds in the ZnO4 tetrahedra respectively [35]. ZTO/C spectrum has observed no noticeable peaks due to fluorescence interference of carbon quantum dots in the composite [36]. This phenomenon occurs when electronic energy of the sample is close to the excitation laser energy. This confirms the composite formation of CQD with ZTO.

The optical properties of the ZTO/C, ZTO and ZTO/ZnO were investigated using UV–Visible DRS spectroscopy. The plot of (αhυ)2 versus hυ of the compounds are shown in Fig. 2b. The optical band gap (Eg) of direct-band gap material was related to the absorbance and photon energy (hυ) by the Kubleka-Munk (K-M) equation: (αhυ)2 = k(hυ − Eg), where, α stands for the absorption coefficient, k is a constant, hυ is the discrete photo energy, and Eg is the band energy gap. This relationship gives the Eg value by extrapolating the straight portion of (αhυ)2 against hυ plot to the point α = 0. The obtained band gap value is 3.69 eV, 3.58 eV and 3.39 eV of ZTO/C, ZTO/ZnO and ZTO respectively. ZTO/C band gap was larger than the ZTO, this could be due to the particle size effect and development of carbon bonding with the ZTO. Similar, increase in band gap of TiO2@ZrO2 due to the development of the Zr-O-C bond and Zr4+ substitution as reported by Sangeetha et al. [37].

SEM and HRTEM images of the prepared ZTO and ZTO/C are shown in fig. S1 and Fig. 3 respectively. HRTEM images of ZTO nanoparticles, shown in Fig. 3a and b depicts particle size of 8–15 nm. The lattice fringe pattern (insert image of Fig. 3b) indicates a lattice interplanar distance of 0.262 nm corresponding to (311) planes of ZTO. The SAED patterns (Fig. 3c) shows the ring pattern of 311, 222, 331, 422 and 440 attributing to high degree of crystallinity of ZTO. Fig. 3d and e shows the HRTEM image of the ZTO/C. The carbon signature on ZTO were observed. The EDS pattern (Fig. 3f) shows the presence of Zn, Sn, O and C elements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BET surface area and pore distribution
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 shows the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and pore size distribution of prepared ZTO/C and ZTO compounds. It is clear from Fig. 4a that ZTO/C and ZTO demonstrate IV isotherm with H2- type hysteresis loops at high relative pressure according to the IUPAC classification, which is a typical characteristic of mesoporous structure [38]. The corresponding pore size distribution, calculated from desorption curve by the Barrett-Joyner Halenda (BJH) method for ZTO/C and ZTO is shown in Fig. 4b. The obtained BET surface area, pore volume and pore diameter are summarized in Table 1 .
The pore diameter and pore volume are observed higher for ZTO/C than ZTO. The carbon particles on the surface of ZTO is reason for higher surface area consequently.

The photocatalytic studies were carried out using prepared ZTO and ZTO/C nanocomposite on direct sunlight radiation to determine the photocatalytic activity on MB dye degradation. Photocatalytic test under direct sunlight were conducted for 3 ppm of MB dye solution with optimized catalytic loading 200 mg per 200 ml dye solution. During the study, dye solutions were collected at equal interval time and subsequently recorded UV–visible spectroscopy to evaluate the degradation. The absorption peaks of MB at 664 nm gradually decreased and vanished in the presence of ZTO/C nanocomposite under direct sunlight radiation at 150 min (refer fig.S2). Photocatalytic degradation efficiency was plotted, Ct/C0 versus time (shown in Fig. 5a). Blank studies were also carried without the catalyst and revealed <5 % of dye degradation. This reveals that the degradation is due to photocatalytic activity of the photocatalyst. ZTO and ZTO/C photocatalysts showed 84 % and 95 % degradation of MB dye in 150 min respectively. Table 2 listed for comparison with various carbon composite catalyst used for MB degradation in visible light. The photodegradation of dyes usually follows pseudo-first-order kinetics and we analysed this behaviour for our reaction studies.
(3)
r = −
dC dt
=
k 0
C
(4)
− ln
C t
C 0
=
k 0
t
where C0 and Ct denotes the initial dye concentration and concentration at time ‘t’ respectively. Plot ln(C0/Ct) vs time for both ZTO and ZTO/C photocatalyst (Fig. 5b) shows linear plot with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.97–0.98, supports that pseudo-first-order kinetics reaction during the degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability and Scavengers studies of the photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The recovered photocatalysts, ZTO/C and ZTO were studied for recyclability for five cycles and the results are depicted in Fig. 5c. The results show 68 % and 73 % efficiency after the fifth cycle, the decline in efficiency of the ZTO/C may be due to carbon leach. However, further study is required to confirm the same. The scavenger test was carried out for ZTO and ZTO/C to identify the reactive species involved in the photocatalysis mechanism by the addition of scavengers such as BQ, KI, KBrO3, and IPA for .O2, h+, e− and .OH respectively [39]. The scavenger studies plot of MB degradation percentage is shown in Fig. 5d. The results show that all the species haven taken part in the degradation mechanism in the both the systems. Whereas in ZTO/C, the degradation efficiencies are more prevented by KBrO3 (50.3 %) and BQ (45.7 %) than IPA (32 %), and KI (29.3 %) which affirms the photoinduced electrons transported to CQD taken apart to enhance the degradation process.

The plausible mechanism for photocatalytic degradation of MB dye utilizing ZTO/C photocatalyst is shown in schematic diagram, Fig. 6 . When ZTO/C was exposed to sunlight, CQDs absorb photons and up-convert to shorter wavelength [40] which then excite ZTO to generate electrons in the conduction band (CB, e
CB
−), and holes in the valence band (VB, h
VB
+) (Eq. (5)). And also, CQDs could collect and transfer photogenerated electron from the conduction band of ZTO thereby hindering the recombination of electron−hole pairs [41].The photoelectron at CQDs will react with O2 to generate superoxide radicals (∙O 2 −), and the holes in the VB oxidize H2O to generate hydroxyl radical (OH−). These free radicals possess high chemical activity which degrade MB to CO2 and H2O (Eqs. (6) to (8)).
(5)
ZTO + hν →
e CB −
+
h VB +
(6)
h VB +
+
H 2
O − − − − →
H +
+
O
.
H
(7)
e CB −
+
O 2
− − − − →
O
. − 2
(8)
O
. − 2
+
O
.
H + MB − − − − → Degraded Product

To conclude, we have successfully synthesized mesoporous pristine ZTO and ZTO/C nanocomposite with high surface area via hydrothermal process using Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) wherein its multiple roles as a reducer and as a component in composite preparation elucidated. The carbon quantum dot modified ZTO enabled higher photocatalytic efficiency owing to its enhanced surface area and preferential orientation onto the ZTO surface which enhanced the interfacial property of the composite. The CQDs present on the ZTO surface embraced the photoinduced electrons transported whereas holes remained in the VB of ZTO, suppressing the recombination probability of photogenerated charge carriers. Moreover, with pseudo-first order kinetics, the separated charge carriers efficiently photo oxidized and reduced the MB molecules, causing it to degrade and decolourise water. The present study reveals the potential approach for CQDs based composites in utilisation for active photocatalytic material and also for reducing and obtaining pure phase ZTO.

Preethi G: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. R. Pillai: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Balan Ramdas: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. S. Ramamoorthy: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Balu Patil: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. I.C. Lekshmi: Formal analysis. Mohankumar Naidu: Formal analysis. Rangaraj L: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2022.109554.
